tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies trigeminal neuralgia in an L0 (diagnosis-only) task so downstream management can be appropriately guided.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'trigeminal neuralgia' and explains it in patient-friendly terms (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Elicits classic features supporting trigeminal neuralgia: recurrent brief (seconds to ~2 minutes) unilateral facial pain, electric shock/stabbing quality, severe intensity, triggered by innocuous stimuli (touch, chewing, brushing teeth, cold air), with pain-free intervals between attacks.
Clarifies pain distribution consistent with trigeminal nerve branches (V2/V3 most common) and laterality; asks about trigger zones.

Screens for red flags/secondary causes that would change urgency or diagnosis: persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, sensory loss/numbness, age <40, progressive neurologic deficits, hearing/vision changes, constitutional symptoms, history of malignancy, immunosuppression, recent trauma/infection.

Considers and documents reasonable differentials verbally (even if not recorded as a tool): dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, sinusitis, cluster headache/TAC, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine, giant cell arteritis (if age-appropriate), multiple sclerosis, intracranial mass/vascular lesion.
Does not prescribe in an L0 task; if discussing treatment, mentions carbamazepine as first-line (communication milestone: explain_treatment) but avoids issuing a prescription via tool.

If discussing medication options, explicitly performs/mentions required safety checks before any prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check (critical safety requirement).

Provides appropriate disposition advice: recommends follow-up and indicates that MRI brain (with attention to trigeminal nerve/posterior fossa) is typically used to evaluate for secondary causes, especially with red flags or atypical features.
Uses clear safety-netting: advises urgent care for new neurologic deficits, facial numbness/weakness, vision changes, severe persistent headache, fever, or rapidly worsening symptoms.
